title: Why we miss places we never lived in
summary: Explore the feeling of longing for a place you have never actually lived in, and consider what it reveals about memory, imagination, and belonging.
topic: Why we miss places we never lived in
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) exploring the feeling of missing a place you have never actually lived in. Describe a specific place that gives you this feeling and try to explain why. Use present perfect, modal verbs, and at least one concession with 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: What surprises me most about this feeling is that it has nothing to do with…
I have never set foot there, yet somehow I feel as if…
If I could explain why certain places pull at us, I would say…

hintVocab.meaningVi: cảm giác khao khát, nhớ nhung
cảm thấy bị thu hút đến
thuộc về một nơi nào đó
lãng mạn hóa một nơi chốn
nỗi nhớ mượn từ người khác

hintVocab.phrase: to carry a place inside you
hintVocab.meaningVi: mang theo một nơi trong lòng
khoảng trống về sự thuộc về
xây dựng một phiên bản của

hintVocab.phrase: to arrive and feel let down
hintVocab.meaningVi: đến nơi và cảm thấy thất vọng

sampleText:
A colleague of mine who grew up in Nairobi once told me that he had always missed Lisbon. He had never been there. He had seen photographs, read a novel set in its tram-lined streets, and listened to fado music late at night, and somehow the city had installed itself in him as a place he had lost, not a place he had never found. I understood him immediately, because I have had the same feeling about Kyoto — a city of temple gardens and quiet mornings that exists in my mind with a precision I cannot justify.

What surprises me most about this feeling is that it has nothing to do with actual memory. We are not missing a place we have been and left; we are missing a version we have constructed from fragments — a photograph, a sentence in a novel, the way light fell in a film. Although this sounds like simple romanticism, I think it points to something deeper. We seem to carry an idea of where we belong before we have any evidence for it. The mind is capable of building a home out of borrowed materials, and it does this quietly, without asking permission. If these imagined places actually existed the way we picture them, we might feel completely at ease the moment we arrived.

However, the feeling is not straightforward longing. There is something pleasant about missing a place you have never lived in, because the loss is clean. Nothing has gone wrong there. No bad days have left a mark. The Lisbon my colleague misses will never disappoint him the way the real one might, and the Kyoto I miss is perpetually still, without crowds or rain or closed temples. Perhaps what we miss is not the place itself but the self we imagine we would have become there — calmer, more rooted, finally at home. That version of us has never been tested, which means it has never failed, and there is a quiet comfort in keeping it that way.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a place he had lost, not a place he had never found
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một nơi anh ấy đã đánh mất, không phải một nơi anh ấy chưa bao giờ tìm thấy
cảm giác khao khát, nhớ nhung
tự cài vào trong anh ấy
một sự rõ ràng mà tôi không thể giải thích
những chất liệu mượn từ nơi khác
cảm thấy hoàn toàn thoải mái
nỗi nhớ đơn giản, rõ ràng
sự mất mát là trong sáng, không vướng bận

sampleAnnotations.phrase: No bad days have left a mark
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Không có ngày tệ nào để lại dấu vết
cuối cùng cảm thấy ở nhà
chưa bao giờ bị thử thách

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for life experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have had the same feeling about Kyoto' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về một trải nghiệm mà người nói đã có và vẫn còn liên quan đến hiện tại. Không có thời điểm cụ thể nào được nhắc đến.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have had the same feeling about Kyoto' uses the present perfect to describe an experience the speaker has lived and that remains relevant now. No specific past time is mentioned.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'although'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although this sounds like simple romanticism, I think it points to something deeper' dùng 'although' để thừa nhận một quan điểm đối lập trước khi trình bày ý chính — đây là cách tạo lập luận cân bằng ở trình độ B1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although this sounds like simple romanticism, I think it points to something deeper' uses 'although' to concede an opposing view before stating the main point — a balanced argument move at B1.

sampleTranslationVi:
Một đồng nghiệp của tôi lớn lên ở Nairobi từng nói với tôi rằng anh ấy luôn nhớ Lisbon. Anh ấy chưa bao giờ đến đó. Anh ấy đã xem những bức ảnh, đọc một cuốn tiểu thuyết lấy bối cảnh ở những con phố có xe điện, và nghe nhạc fado vào đêm khuya, và bằng cách nào đó thành phố đó đã tự cài vào trong anh như một nơi anh đã mất, không phải một nơi anh chưa bao giờ tìm thấy. Tôi hiểu ngay điều anh ấy nói, vì tôi cũng có cảm giác tương tự về Kyoto — một thành phố có những khu vườn đền chùa và những buổi sáng yên tĩnh tồn tại trong tâm trí tôi với một sự rõ ràng mà tôi không thể giải thích.

Điều khiến tôi ngạc nhiên nhất về cảm giác này là nó không liên quan gì đến ký ức thực sự. Chúng ta không nhớ một nơi chúng ta đã đến và rời đi; chúng ta nhớ một phiên bản mà chúng ta đã xây dựng từ những mảnh ghép — một bức ảnh, một câu trong tiểu thuyết, cách ánh sáng chiếu trong một bộ phim. Mặc dù nghe có vẻ chỉ là sự lãng mạn đơn thuần, tôi nghĩ nó chỉ ra điều gì đó sâu sắc hơn. Chúng ta dường như mang theo ý niệm về nơi mình thuộc về trước khi có bất kỳ bằng chứng nào. Tâm trí có khả năng xây dựng một ngôi nhà từ những chất liệu mượn từ nơi khác, và nó làm điều này âm thầm, mà không xin phép. Nếu những nơi tưởng tượng này thực sự tồn tại theo cách chúng ta hình dung, chúng ta có thể cảm thấy hoàn toàn thoải mái ngay khoảnh khắc đặt chân đến.

Tuy nhiên, cảm giác này không đơn giản chỉ là nỗi nhớ. Có điều gì đó thú vị khi nhớ một nơi bạn chưa bao giờ sống, vì sự mất mát là trong sáng. Không có điều gì tồi tệ xảy ra ở đó. Không có ngày tệ nào để lại dấu vết. Lisbon mà đồng nghiệp tôi nhớ sẽ không bao giờ làm anh thất vọng như Lisbon thật có thể, và Kyoto mà tôi nhớ luôn luôn tĩnh lặng, không có đám đông hay mưa hay những ngôi đền đóng cửa. Có lẽ điều chúng ta nhớ không phải là bản thân nơi đó mà là con người chúng ta tưởng tượng mình sẽ trở thành ở đó — bình thản hơn, có gốc rễ hơn, cuối cùng cảm thấy ở nhà. Phiên bản đó của chúng ta chưa bao giờ bị thử thách, nghĩa là nó chưa bao giờ thất bại, và có một sự an ủi nhẹ nhàng khi giữ nguyên điều đó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a life experience?
options: I have had the same feeling about Kyoto for years.
I have had the same feeling about Kyoto since last Tuesday.
I had the same feeling about Kyoto since I was a child.
I have had the same feeling about Kyoto when I was young.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect + 'for years' describes an ongoing experience without a fixed past point.
mcQuestions.hint: The present perfect pairs naturally with 'for' + period of time for ongoing experience.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly as a concession?
options: Although this sounds like simple romanticism, I think it points to something deeper.
Although this sounds like simple romanticism, but I think it points to something deeper.
Although this sounds like simple romanticism so I think it points to something deeper.
This sounds like simple romanticism, although because it points to something deeper.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no additional conjunction like 'but' or 'so' is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' already signals contrast; adding 'but' creates a double conjunction error.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a modal to express a hypothetical present outcome?
options: If these imagined places actually existed the way we picture them, we might feel completely at ease.
If these imagined places actually existed the way we picture them, we will feel completely at ease.
If these imagined places actually exist the way we picture them, we might felt completely at ease.
If these imagined places actually existed the way we picture them, we might to feel completely at ease.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: 'if + past simple, modal + bare infinitive' for hypothetical present situations.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional uses 'if + past simple' and 'would/might/could + bare infinitive'.

options: However, the feeling is not straightforward longing.
However the feeling is not straightforward longing.
The feeling is not straightforward longing, however it is complex.
The feeling, however is not straightforward longing.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'however' begins a sentence as a discourse connector, it is followed by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: 'However' at the start of a sentence needs a comma immediately after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: A city that exists in my mind with a precision I cannot justify.
A city which it exists in my mind with a precision I cannot justify.
A city that exists in my mind with a precision that I cannot justify it.
A city exists in my mind with a precision I cannot to justify.
mcQuestions.explanation: Object relative pronouns can be omitted ('a precision [that] I cannot justify'); no pronoun repetition needed.
mcQuestions.hint: Do not repeat the pronoun after the relative clause verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly expresses parallel ideas using 'not … but …'?
options: Not the place itself but the self we imagine we would have become there.
Not the place itself but the self we imagine we will became there.
Not the place itself, but the self that we imagined we would became there.
Not the place itself but the self we are imagining we would have become there.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not X but Y' requires the same grammatical category on each side; 'would have become' is correct past conditional.
mcQuestions.hint: Check that both sides of 'not … but …' are the same grammatical type.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word best fills the blank: 'The city has ___ itself in him as a place he had lost'?
options: installed
install
installing
been installed
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'has installed itself' uses the past participle; reflexive 'itself' makes it active, not passive.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect active = has/have + past participle; reflexive verbs are active.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whereas' to contrast two ideas correctly?
options: Whereas real places can disappoint, imagined ones stay perfect.
Whereas real places can disappoint but imagined ones stay perfect.
Real places can disappoint whereas, imagined ones stay perfect.
Whereas, real places can disappoint imagined ones stay perfect.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whereas + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'whereas' and no extra conjunction.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whereas' introduces a contrasting clause; the main clause follows with a comma separating them.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly describes an ongoing feeling using present perfect continuous?
options: I have been carrying this image of the city for as long as I can remember.
I have carried this image of the city for as long as I can remember.
I am carrying this image of the city for as long as I can remember.
I was carrying this image of the city for as long as I can remember.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect continuous ('have been carrying') emphasises the ongoing, unfinished nature of the action.
mcQuestions.hint: Use present perfect continuous when the action started in the past and is still ongoing.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is the most appropriate B1 concluding statement?
options: That version of us has never been tested, which means it has never failed.
That version of us never been tested, which means it never failed.
That version of us has never been tested, which mean it has never failed.
That version of us have never been tested, which means it has never failed.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third person singular 'has never been tested' (passive present perfect) and 'means' are both correctly conjugated.
mcQuestions.hint: Third person singular takes 'has' for present perfect and 's' on the main verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best uses 'while' to contrast two situations?
options: While some people miss places they have left, others miss places they have never seen.
While some people miss places they have left, but others miss places they have never seen.
While some people are missing places they left, others are missing places they never saw.
Some people miss places they have left while, others miss places they have never seen.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'While + clause, main clause' contrasts two ideas without needing an additional conjunction.
mcQuestions.hint: 'While' for contrast works like 'although' — don't add 'but' after the comma.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Can you think of a place that exists mainly in songs, films, or books — somewhere many people feel they 'know' without having visited? Why do shared cultural images of a place create such a powerful emotional pull?
